interactions.summary: Jiang summarizes the damage of meritocracy: mental illness, lost mobility, 1% wealth concentration, political division, corruption, identity destruction, mismanagement, and a soulless elite. He then opens the floor for questions.
A student asks whether there is a way to get rid of the bad impacts and develop outside the system.

Jiang says the structural solution is to destroy or publicize the Ivy League, but since that will not happen, individuals should recognize the system as evil, focus on real learning, embrace failure, reflection, resilience, and unstructured time.

speaker_notes.note: Single-word UNKNOWN “Okay?” is likely filler or diarization noise, not a substantive speaker.

claims.claim: Jiang attributes a broad crisis bundle to meritocracy: mental illness, social immobility, 1% wealth concentration, left-right division, corruption, identity breakdown, elite mismanagement, and soulless leadership.
The best structural solution would be to destroy the Ivy League or make it public so its private institutional power and secrecy are broken.
Because the Ivy League is too powerful to be destroyed soon, the individual response is to see the system clearly and choose real learning over indoctrinated success-seeking.
Real success before meritocracy depended on open-mindedness, failure, reflection, resilience, learning, and time for oneself; grade competition destroys each of those conditions.

signature_moments.moment: The summary lands as a sarcastic funeral toast to American meritocracy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: thank you, American meritocracy, for destroying the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the lecture’s bitter public punchline.

signature_moments.moment: The real solution is to destroy the Ivy League.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the Q&A a structural answer before the personal advice.

signature_moments.moment: Failure is not a blemish but the best teacher; meritocracy removes it and therefore removes reflection.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It defines Jiang’s alternative developmental model.

signature_moments.moment: Every minute being organized is described as intentional anti-reflection.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You don’t even have time to just think for yourself
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It links overscheduling to social control.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Learning grounded in honesty, failure, reflection, resilience, and open-mindedness rather than grades or institutional reward.
Not yet named here, but prepared as the non-reward mode of connection, reflection, and creativity.
Making elite universities public/government-controlled to reduce private institutional power and secrecy.

uncertainty_notes.note: Diarization marks an UNKNOWN one-word filler; no public interaction should be built from it.
